should_trigger: Design an auto-orchestrator for five coding agents that share two test environments, can deadlock on locks, and must release every reserved resource after success or failure.
Our workflow has queues, retries, approvals, and parallel branches. Model it with places, transitions, tokens, markings, and firing rules so we can find deadlocks.
A tool-runner sometimes consumes the same budget permit twice or leaves jobs stuck after cancellation. Use a formal token model to repair the orchestration.
Create a PNML-compatible process model for an agent workflow and call out which net type, extensions, and namespace assumptions affect tool portability.
should_not_trigger: Rank these roadmap ideas by revenue impact and customer effort.
Summarize the history of Petri nets in one paragraph without applying them to a workflow.
Make a simple ordered checklist for publishing a newsletter; there is no concurrency, queue, resource, or state explosion.
edge_cases: The workflow has only four steps, but one step acquires a lock and another may fail before releasing it. Decide whether a Petri-net model is useful or a direct state-machine repair is enough.
We need to model rich task payloads and user-specific metadata moving through the workflow. Decide whether to use a colored/high-level net or keep data predicates as external guards.
The team wants custom reset and inhibitor arcs in PNML. Explain the portability risk and whether a standard Place/Transition net can express the orchestration safely enough.
